coredata.dc:title: Machine learning-assisted fluorescence visualization for sequential quantitative detection of aluminum and fluoride ions
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Research Center for Eco-Environmental Sciences, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Research Center for Eco-Environmental Sciences, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Qiang
Li, Xin
Yu, Long
Wang, Lingxiao
Wen, Zhiqing
Su, Pengchen
Sun, Zhenli
Wang, Suhua
coredata.dc:description: The presence of aluminum (Al3+) and fluoride (F−) ions in the environment can be harmful to ecosystems and human health, highlighting the need for accurate and efficient monitoring. In this paper, an innovative approach is presented that leverages the power of machine learning to enhance the accuracy and efficiency of fluorescence-based detection for sequential quantitative analysis of aluminum (Al3+) and fluoride (F−) ions in aqueous solutions. The proposed method involves the synthesis of sulfur-functionalized carbon dots (C-dots) as fluorescence probes, with fluorescence enhancement upon interaction with Al3+ ions, achieving a detection limit of 4.2 nmol/L. Subsequently, in the presence of F− ions, fluorescence is quenched, with a detection limit of 47.6 nmol/L. The fingerprints of fluorescence images are extracted using a cross-platform computer vision library in Python, followed by data preprocessing. Subsequently, the fingerprint data is subjected to cluster analysis using the K-means model from machine learning, and the average Silhouette Coefficient indicates excellent model performance. Finally, a regression analysis based on the principal component analysis method is employed to achieve more precise quantitative analysis of aluminum and fluoride ions. The results demonstrate that the developed model excels in terms of accuracy and sensitivity. This groundbreaking model not only showcases exceptional performance but also addresses the urgent need for effective environmental monitoring and risk assessment, making it a valuable tool for safeguarding our ecosystems and public health.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Machine learning
Aluminum ion detection
Fluorine ion detection
Fluorescence probe
K-means model

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Research on the fluorescence response of Al3+ and F−
Regression analysis based on PCA method

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Machine learning classification and quantitative model based on fluorescence fingerprint information.
(a) TEM image of MCDs (The internal illustration shows the statistics of particle size distribution). (b) FTIR spectra of MCDs. (c-f) XPS spectra of C1s, N1s, O1s, and S2p.
(a) UV–Vis absorption (red line), FL excitation (EX, black line), and emission (EM, blue line) of as-prepared MCDs. (b) Excitation-emission-matrix spectrum of MCDs.

(a) Fluorescence titration curve of MCDs with the addition of Al3+ (0–24 µmol/L). (b) The linear relationships between F/F 0 and the concentration of Al3+. (c) Fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F 0) responses of MCDs for various metal ions. (d) Response of MCDs to Al3+ under the interference of other metal ions (110 µmol/L).

(a) Fluorescence titration curve of MCDs-Al3+ with the addition of F− (0–75 µmol/L). (b) The linear relationships between (F 0-F)/F 0 and the concentration of F−. (c) Fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F 0) responses of MCDs-Al3+ for various anions. (d) Response of MCDs-Al3+ to F− under the interference of other anions (380 µmol/L).
(a) UV–vis absorption titration curve of MCDs with the addition of Al3+ (0–60 µmol/L). (b) FTIR spectrum of MCDs and MCDs+Al3+. (c) The high-resolution XPS patterns of F1s.

Statistical distribution plots of the Al3+-ion fluorescence detection sample data. (a) Violin plot showing the data distribution of RGB feature values. (b) Box plots depicting the data distribution of the three feature variables under different concentration conditions. (c) Violin plot displaying the data distribution of RGB feature values after standardization. (d) Box plots illustrate the data distribution of the three feature variables after standardization under different concentration conditions.

K-means clustering analysis and model evaluation result plots. (a) and (b) represent cluster number display plots for samples in the Al3+ and F− detection experiments, respectively. (c) and (d) depict two-dimensional scatter plots of sample clustering for the Al3+ and F− detection experiments, respectively. (e) and (f) show silhouette information plots for clustering in the Al3+ and F− detection experiments, respectively.

Regression analysis results based on the principal component analysis method. (a) and (d) respectively show the number of principal components and the corresponding variance explained in the Al3+ and F− detection. (b) and (e) display correlation heatmaps between two principal components and concentration variables in the Al3+ and F− detection experiments. (c) and (f) are linear regression analysis plots for the first principal component and concentration in the Al3+ and F− detection experiments, respectively.

head.ce:title: Machine learning-assisted fluorescence visualization for sequential quantitative detection of aluminum and fluoride ions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Guangdong University of Petrochemical Technology, Maoming 525000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aSchool of Environmental Science and Engineering, Guangdong University of Petrochemical Technology, Maoming 525000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: MOE Key Laboratory of Resources and Environmental System Optimization, College of Environmental Science and Engineering, North China Electric Power University, Beijing 102206, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: MOE Key Laboratory of Resources and Environmental System Optimization
College of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bMOE Key Laboratory of Resources and Environmental System Optimization, College of Environmental Science and Engineering, North China Electric Power University, Beijing, 102206, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The presence of aluminum (Al3+) and fluoride (F−) ions in the environment can be harmful to ecosystems and human health, highlighting the need for accurate and efficient monitoring. In this paper, an innovative approach is presented that leverages the power of machine learning to enhance the accuracy and efficiency of fluorescence-based detection for sequential quantitative analysis of aluminum (Al3+) and fluoride (F−) ions in aqueous solutions. The proposed method involves the synthesis of sulfur-functionalized carbon dots (C-dots) as fluorescence probes, with fluorescence enhancement upon interaction with Al3+ ions, achieving a detection limit of 4.2 nmol/L. Subsequently, in the presence of F− ions, fluorescence is quenched, with a detection limit of 47.6 nmol/L. The fingerprints of fluorescence images are extracted using a cross-platform computer vision library in Python, followed by data preprocessing. Subsequently, the fingerprint data is subjected to cluster analysis using the K-means model from machine learning, and the average Silhouette Coefficient indicates excellent model performance. Finally, a regression analysis based on the principal component analysis method is employed to achieve more precise quantitative analysis of aluminum and fluoride ions. The results demonstrate that the developed model excels in terms of accuracy and sensitivity. This groundbreaking model not only showcases exceptional performance but also addresses the urgent need for effective environmental monitoring and risk assessment, making it a valuable tool for safeguarding our ecosystems and public health.

ce:section.ce:para: The detection of aluminum (Al3+) and fluoride (F−) ions holds immense significance due to their widespread presence in natural waters and their potential health and environmental impacts (Kim and Gabbai, 2009; Purkait et al., 2018; Solanki et al., 2022). As a neurotoxic substance with a strong affinity for brain and hippocampal tissue, Al3+ accumulates easily and is not easily removed, resulting in neuronal toxicity and a significant reduction in learning ability. Excessive intake of Al3+ by pregnant women can not only damage themselves but can even cause abnormal development of the fetus (MacDonald et al., 1987; Pi et al., 2019). According to the World Health Organization (WHO), the maximum permissible concentration of Al3+ ions in drinking water is 200 µg/L (7.41 µmol/L) (Hou et al., 2020). Fluoride, on the other hand, is added to water supplies to prevent dental cavities (Diao et al., 2016). However, excessive fluoride intake can lead to dental and skeletal fluorosis, affecting dental and bone health (Hu et al., 2018). Research findings have consistently indicated that consuming water with fluoride (F−) concentrations below 1.5 mg/L is considered safe. Numerous studies have established this threshold as an acceptable level for drinking water, ensuring minimal risk of adverse health effects related to excessive fluoride intake (Grzegorzek et al., 2020; Lacson et al., 2021). Therefore, accurate and sensitive detection of Al3+ and F− ions is crucial for environmental monitoring, ensuring safe drinking water, and safeguarding public health. Moreover, it aids in understanding the behavior and distribution of these ions in different environments, contributing to better resource management and environmental protection efforts.

Presently, a range of well-established methods exist for the detection of Al3+ and F− ions in various samples. These methods encompass atomic fluorescence (Unniram Parambil et al., 2022), spectrophotometry (Zhou et al., 2014), complex titration (Pesavento and Alberti, 2000), atomic absorption (Nguyen et al., 2004), and inductively coupled plasma atomic emission mass spectrometry (ICP-AES) (Rezaee et al., 2010; Manzoori and Miyazaki, 1990). Each technique offers unique advantages and sensitivities, making them valuable tools for specific applications. However, these traditional detection methods often involve time-consuming and labor-intensive procedures, which may limit their practicality in real-time monitoring applications. In contrast, the integration of machine learning algorithms with fluorescence visualization offers a powerful and efficient approach to analyze complex data and accurately detect the presence of these metal ions (Dong et al., 2023; Li et al., 2022). The use of machine learning algorithms allows for effective feature extraction and pattern recognition from the fluorescence signals generated by the carbon dots (MCDs) upon interaction with aluminum and fluoride ions. By harnessing the power of data-driven decision-making, the developed models can achieve unparalleled sensitivity in detecting even trace amounts of these metal ions, making them ideal candidates for real-time and low-concentration monitoring applications.

In this study, we presented an innovative fluorescence-based optical nanosensor operating in an "off-on-off" mode for sensitive detection of aluminum ions (Al3+) and fluoride ions (F−) in water. The detection limits were found to be 4.2 nmol/L and 47.6 nmol/L, respectively. We present a novel approach that combines machine learning techniques with fluorescence visualization for the sequential quantitative detection of Al3+ and F− ions (Scheme 1 ). By capitalizing on the inherent fluorescence properties of specific materials, we can acquire real-time visual representations of the presence and concentration of these metal ions. Through the integration of machine learning algorithms, our objective is to bolster the accuracy and sensitivity of this detection method, rendering it applicable to a diverse array of fields, including environmental monitoring, industrial process control, and biomedical research.

The reagents and relevant laboratory equipment employed for the probe synthesis can be found in Appendix A Text S1. The functionalized carbon dots were synthesized using a solvent-thermal method, with detailed synthesis and separation steps provided in Appendix A Text S2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Research on the fluorescence response of Al3+ and F−
ce:section.ce:para: Before conducting fluorescence visualization detection, we tested the probe's performance in detecting aluminum ions and fluoride ions using a fluorescence spectrometer (Appendix A Text S3).

After validating the fluorescence detection performance of the probe, we collected fluorescence visualization images of aluminum ions and fluoride ions at various concentrations. Subsequently, we utilized Python to extract fingerprint information from these images. For detailed procedures, please refer to Appendix A Text S4. Subsequently, we matched these feature values with the corresponding concentration labels, forming two datasets. Therefore, each dataset contains 60 data points with three features and six concentration target values (Appendix A Table S1).

Using the R programming language and the 'cluster' package, we established clustering models. Firstly, we employed the Gap statistic algorithm to compute the optimal number of clusters. Then, we set random seeds and determined the number of clusters (k) and the number of initial points (nstar) in the K-means method to build the model. Finally, we assessed the clustering performance using the silhouette function to calculate the silhouette coefficient. Additionally, before the clustering analysis, we evaluated cluster tendency for both datasets using the Hopkins Statistic. Following this, we utilized the 'princomp' function in R to create a principal component analysis (PCA) model, reducing the feature dimensions of the dataset. We then established linear regression models for the newly generated principal component variables using the 'lm' function, allowing us to explore the quantitative relationships between the principal components and concentration variables.

To better investigate the properties of the synthesized carbon nanoparticles, a series of characterization experiments were carried out. The morphology and structure of the prepared MCDs were analyzed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As can be seen in Fig. 1 a, the MCDs are completely dispersed and have uniform particles, the illustration shows that the lattice spacing of the particles is 0.202 nm, and additionally, their diameters were calculated to be in the range of 0.63–2.58 nm, with an average particle size of 1.64 nm and conforming to a Gaussian distribution. In addition, for a better view of the morphology of the MCDs, clear and regular particles can be seen from the high-resolution TEM (Appendix A Fig. S1).

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) for evaluating the important components of MCDs (Fig. 1b). The absorption bands observed at 3000–3500 cm−1 were attributed to the tensile vibrations of OH and NH. The peak located at 2781 cm−1 indicated that the surface of MCDs contains methylene. The peak at 1722 cm−1 was related to the stretching vibration of C=O, while the peaks at 1468 cm−1 and 1397 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration of C=C and C-N. The peak at 1052 cm−1 was due to the stretching vibration of C-O, while the spikes at 769 cm−1 and 534 cm−1 indicated the presence of C-S and S-S, respectively (Wu et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2019). Further investigation was conducted on the elemental composition of MCDs through X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) analysis. As shown in Appendix A Fig. S2, the XPS of MCDs had four major peaks at 163.3, 284.5, 400.0, and 531.8 eV, corresponding to peaks at S2p, C1s, N1s, and O1s respectively. These results indicated that MCDs were composed of C (59.54%), N (9.18%), O (25.41%) and S (5.87%). In the high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s in MCDs (Fig. 1c), the presence of C-C/C=C, C-N, and C=O/C=N was confirmed by three peaks centered at 284.5 eV, 286.1 eV, and 288.8 eV, respectively. The N1s peaks at 400.0 eV and 405.3 eV indicated that nitrogen was mainly in the form of N-H, N-O/N=O (Fig. 1d) (Yang et al., 2019; Bayda et al., 2017). The O1s band could split into two peaks at 531.6 eV and 532.9 eV, respectively attributed to C=O and C-O (Fig. 1e). Notably, we found four peaks in the high-resolution spectrum of S 2p, namely S 2p3/2 (163.3 eV), S 2p1/2 (164.4 eV), -C-SO2- (167.7 eV) and -C-SO3- (168.8 eV) (Fig. 1f) (Chen et al., 2021; Li et al., 2019). Taken together, XPS and FTIR results indicate that the prepared MCDs contain abundant functional groups.

To fully explore the optical properties of MCDs, fluorescence spectra and UV visible (UV–Vis) spectra of MCDs were recorded for the first time. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the UV–Vis spectrum of MCDs exhibited an absorption peak at 312 nm, which was attributed to the absorption band generated by n - π* transitions (Zhang et al., 2019). In addition, it can be seen that the optimal excitation and emission of MCDs are 264 nm and 485 nm, respectively. Similarly, the above conclusion was also verified in the emission-excitation-matrix spectrum, where it was observed that the center of the red contour line corresponds exactly to the intersection point of optimal excitation (264 nm) and optimal emission (485 nm), as shown in Fig. 2b. Then, we investigated the stability of the synthesized probe before and after the reaction (Appendix A Fig. S3). After continuous irradiation for 60 min, we found no significant change in the fluorescence of the probe, indicating that the synthesized probe has satisfactory optical stability.

To explore the fluorescence response of MCDs to Al3+, different concentrations of Al3+ were added to equivalent solutions of MCDs, and the FL intensity of MCDs was plotted with the concentration of Al3+ (Fig. 3 a). In detail, by adding 20 µL of probe stock solution to a fluorescent cuvette containing 2 mL of phosphate buffer solution (pH = 7.4), to which (0–24 µmol/L) Al3+ was gradually added, a significant increase in fluorescence intensity was observed, and within the 1–22 µmol/L concentration range, the Al3+ concentration showed a satisfactory linear relationship with F/F 0: y = 0.2236x + 0.7881, R 2 value (0.9908) approximating to 1 (Fig. 3b). Additionally, the detection limit (LOD) was 4.2 nmol/L (LOD = 3σ/k, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank measurements, and k is the slope of the calibration curve line).

To better reflect the fluorescence specificity of MCDs for Al3+, some common metal ions (Ca2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, K+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Na+, Zn2+) were selected as controls. As shown in Fig. 3c, when Al3+ and other common metal ions were added to the same MCDs solution, only the MCDs solution containing Al3+ showed significant fluorescence changes, while the MCDs solution bound to other common metal ions did not show any fluorescence changes. Fig. 3d shows the fluorescence response of MCDs to Al3+ in the presence of interference from other metal ions. It can be seen from the graph that the other common metal ions do not interfere significantly with Al3+. These results support the conclusion that MCDs have good sensitivity, selectivity, and interference resistance for Al3+ detection and can be used for fluorescence sensing.

Surprisingly, when a series of common anions (F−, Br−, Cl−, CO3 2−, HCO3 −, I−, S2−, SCN−, SO4 2−) were added to the Al3+-MCDs system, the introduction of F− resulted in a significant quenching of the fluorescence of the Al3+-MCDs system (Fig. 4 a). This may be due to the competitive binding of F− with Al3+ and the formation of a complex. The probe Al3+-MCDs returned to the monomer form, resulting in a significant disappearance of fluorescence in the solution. As shown in Fig. 4b, F− was measured at concentrations of 3–60 µmol/L showing a good linear relationship with (F 0-F)/F 0: y = 0.0141x - 0.0021, R 2 = 0.9853, and the LOD is as low as 47.6 nmol/L. However, under the same conditions, the addition of other common anions does not cause the same optical changes (Fig. 4c). To better highlight the anti-interference ability of the Al3+-MCDs system against F−, other anions with a concentration of 5 times were added to the system separately and then detected for F−. From Fig. 4d, the system has good anti-interference performance when detecting F−. The above phenomenon indicates that the Al3+-MCDs system can be used as a new probe system for in situ identification of F−.

To investigate the possible binding modes between MCDs and Al3+, a mechanistic analysis was carried out. Based on the fact that the strong vibration of the S-S bond in the synthetic MCDs consumes the energy of the excited state, leading to their almost non-fluorescence, the addition of Al3+ breaks this vibrational state (Xu et al., 2023). As can be seen in Fig. 5 a, the UV–vis absorption peak gradually decreases and appears to shift to the left with the addition of Al3+. This result demonstrates a significant interaction between Al3+ and MCDs, which is presumed to be responsible for the disruption of the rigid structure of MCDs and the generation of aggregation-induced luminescence (AIE) (Jalili and Khataee, 2018). Further, we compared the changes in the FTIR spectra before and after the addition of Al3+ (Fig. 5b). The CO bond at 1052 cm−1 and the C-S bond at 769 cm−1 showed significant vibrational changes after the addition of Al3+, which is presumed to be the cause of the AIE. In addition, we also considered the changes in XPS spectra after the addition of Al3+. Compared to Appendix A Fig. S2, the intensity of the C1s peak in Appendix A Fig. S4 significantly decreased, indicating a deterioration in the structure of the probe and a decrease in crystallinity. The changes in the high-resolution O 1s and S 2p spectra (Appendix A Figs. S5 and Fig. S6) indicate a decrease in the content of CO bonds and groups containing S bonds, respectively. The above phenomenon indicates that the results are consistent with those of infrared spectroscopy, further confirming that the addition of Al3+ causes the AIE effect, thereby enhancing the fluorescence of the probe.

For the reaction between MCDs- Al3+ and F−, which was also explored, Al3+ and F− have a strong affinity for each other and form complexes very readily (Fu et al., 2020). The formation of AlF3 complexes can be seen from the high-resolution XPS patterns of F 1s (Fig. 5c) (Chang et al., 2019; Berisha et al., 2016; Tian, 2017). Similarly, the phenomenon was verified by the spiking of Al3+ and F − in fluorescence titration experiments in amounts close to 1:3. The above results indicate that the addition of F− gradually pulls Al3+ out of the MCDs- Al3+ complex, releasing free MCDs and causing fluorescence quenching in the solution, thus successfully achieving an effective way to detect both ions based on the "off-on-off" mode. We compared this probe with other studies and found that the material we synthesized had good performance (Appendix A Table S2).

Before establishing the models, we utilized the 'scale' function in the R programming language to standardize both datasets separately (Appendix A Table S3). Subsequently, we created violin plots and box plots for the Al3+ and F− datasets, providing a clear understanding of the distribution status and trends in the data before and after standardization. Fig. 6 a displays the interval distribution of RGB values in the fluorescence images, with the variation in the Blue feature value aligning with the changes in blue fluorescence observed in the experiments. In Fig. 6b, it is evident that as the concentration of Al3+ ions increased, the three fluorescence feature values also increased gradually. The data distribution for each concentration label is relatively concentrated, with hardly any outliers. Among the three features, the Blue feature exhibits the largest enhancement and variation. Fig. 6c and d depict the standardized data, showing that the data distribution remains unchanged. The three feature data points are all distributed within the same range of −2 to 2, enhancing comparability between different feature dimensions. In the detection experiment for F− ions, a similar standardization and analysis of the data were carried out (Appendix A Fig. S7).

After standardization, both datasets exhibit continuous features, with samples distributed similarly across dimensions. The variances are equal, the samples are independent, and no outliers are present. Therefore, they are suitable for analysis using the K-means model. Calculations show that the Hopkins Statistic values for both datasets are greater than 0.7 (Hopkins Statistic (Al3+) = 0.96, Hopkins Statistic (F−) = 0.95), indicating a high clustering tendency in both datasets. Additionally, three-dimensional scatter plots of the samples (Appendix A Fig. S8) and correlation heatmaps (Appendix A Fig. S9) were created to understand the spatial distribution and clustering of the data. It can be observed that samples with the same concentration exhibit strong correlations. In the Al3+ detection experiment, the optimal number of clusters calculated by the model is 6 groups (Fig. 7 a), matching the number of concentration groups we configured for the respective ions. The results of the clustering analysis are shown in Fig. 7c, and a comparison with the labeled sample names (Appendix A Fig. S10a) reveals that the clusters represent samples with the same concentration. This indicates that samples with the same concentration have similar color features. The subsequently calculated average Silhouette Coefficient is 0.74 (Fig. 7e), indicating a good clustering result. In the F− detection experiment, which is similar to the Al3+ detection, the optimal number of clusters is also 6 (Fig. 7b), with each cluster composed of samples of the same concentration (Fig. 7d, Appendix A Fig. S10b). The average Silhouette Coefficient is 0.74 (Fig. 7f), further confirming the effectiveness of the K-means clustering method in visually sensing and recognizing concentration. This has practical significance in achieving wide-concentration sample detection and automated classification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Regression analysis based on PCA method
ce:section.ce:para: Before the regression analysis, a correlation analysis was conducted between the feature variables and the concentration variables. The results indicate that there is a significant correlation among all the variables (Appendix A Fig. S11). Therefore, we employed principal component analysis (PCA) to reduce data dimensionality and eliminate the mutual influences between the original data components. Based on the analysis results, a variable correlation circle plot was generated (Appendix A Fig. S12), demonstrating that the correlation of the three feature values from both datasets with the principal axes is greater than 0.99, and they are positively correlated with PC1. Additionally, scree plots were created (Fig. 8 a, d), showing that the first principal component explains more than 97% of the variance in both experimental datasets. Consequently, the three feature variables were replaced by a one-dimensional variable, PC1, reducing the data dimensionality from three-dimensional variables to one-dimensional variables. To further investigate the quantitative relationship between the principal component and concentration variables, correlation heatmaps were separately generated for the Al3+ and F− detection experiments. Fig. 8b illustrates that there is a significant positive correlation between PC1 and Conc. (r PC1 = 0.96) in the case of Al3+ detection, while the correlation between PC2 and Conc. is low (r PC2 = 0.18). In Fig. 8e, it is evident that in the F− experiment, PC1 exhibits a significant negative correlation with Conc. (r PC1 = −0.99). We established linear relationships between the independent variable (PC1) and the dependent variable (Conc.) separately for both experiments. Utilizing the training dataset, we constructed linear regression models. Subsequently, based on the fitted functions, we predicted concentrations for the test dataset. As depicted in Fig. 8c and f, the fitted regression lines and the predictions for the test dataset indicate that the predicted concentrations closely match the actual concentration values. This demonstrates that there is a linear relationship between PC1 and Conc. Therefore, the use of feature principal components in regression analysis holds significant guidance for pollutant concentration detection.

In conclusion, our study has introduced a groundbreaking approach to the sequential quantitative detection of aluminum (Al3+) and fluoride (F−) ions in aqueous solutions, leveraging the capabilities of machine learning to significantly enhance the accuracy and efficiency of fluorescence-based detection methods. This research not only addresses the critical need for precise and efficient monitoring of these ions in environmental and health contexts but also showcases the transformative potential of machine learning in enhancing fluorescence-based analytical techniques. As a result, our work offers valuable insights into the development of future monitoring and detection methodologies, marking a significant step toward improved ion detection and quantification across various domains.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. U21A20290), Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (No. 2022A1515011656), the Projects of Talents Recruitment of GDUPT (No. 2023rcyj1003), the 2022 “Sail Plan” Project of Maoming Green Chemical Industry Research Institute (No. MMGCIRI2022YFJH-Y-024), and Maoming Science and Technology Project (No. 2023382).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.jes.2024.01.023.
